perturbation.original_text: 1.2. “Confidential Information” means information related to a Party, its Group Companies, its Authorized Persons, this Agreement or any agreement to which this Agreement is annexed or upon which this Agreement’s Purpose is based , that a Party receives or accesses , except information that (i) is or falls into the public domain without fault of the Receiving Party , (ii) the Receiving Party can prove was in its possession without any obligation of confidentiality prior to receipt from the Disclosing Party , (iii) is independently developed by the Receiving Party , or (iv) is obtained by the Receiving Party from third parties without any obligation of confidentiality to the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.2. “Confidential Information” means information related to a Party, its Group Companies, its Authorized Persons, this Agreement or any agreement to which this Agreement is annexed or upon which this Agreement’s Purpose is based , that a Party receives or accesses , including information that (i) is or falls into the public domain without fault of the Receiving Party , (ii) the Receiving Party can prove was in its possession without any obligation of confidentiality prior to receipt from the Disclosing Party , (iii) is independently developed by the Receiving Party , or (iv) is obtained by the Receiving Party from third parties without any obligation of confidentiality to the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the 'except' clause, the definition of 'Confidential Information' now includes information already in the public domain. This contradicts standard legal interpretations of confidentiality.
perturbation.law_explanation: The definition of 'trade secret' (and, by extension, 'confidential information' in many contexts) explicitly excludes information generally known or readily ascertainable. Including publicly available information in the contractual definition of 'Confidential Information' expands it beyond legal recognition.

Definitions As used in this chapter- (1) the term "foreign instrumentality" means any agency, bureau, ministry, component, institution, association, or any legal, commercial, or business organization, corporation, firm, or entity that is substantially owned, controlled, sponsored, commanded, managed, or dominated by a foreign government; (2) the term "foreign agent" means any officer, employee, proxy, servant, delegate, or representative of
perturbation.justification: The modification to the contract text creates a contradiction by including information that is already in the public domain within the definition of 'Confidential Information.' This contradicts the conventional understanding of confidential information, which generally excludes publicly available knowledge. 18 USC 1839, defines terms related to trade secrets, and while it does not explicitly define 'confidential information' or directly exclude public information from its definition, the Law Explanation highlights that trade secrets, and by extension confidential information, typically exclude information generally known or readily ascertainable. The removal of the 'except' clause now makes the definition of Confidential Information include things in the public domain.

perturbation.original_text: 3.5. Information Security . Each Party shall secure the other Party’s Confidential Information and protect it from access by anyone other than its Authorized Persons, in the same way that it protects its own Confidential Information, but in all cases using a professional degree of care . The Receiv ing Party’s obligations relating to securing and protecting Confidential Information shall remain in effect for the later of either (i) 5 (five) years after the last date defined in clause 3.2 or (ii) until the Confidential Information may reasonably be considered as no longer confidential.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.5. Information Security . Each Party shall secure the other Party’s Confidential Information and protect it from access by anyone other than its Authorized Persons, in the same way that it protects its own Confidential Information, using whatever degree of care it deems appropriate. The Receiving Party’s obligations relating to securing and protecting Confidential Information shall remain in effect for the later of either (i) 5 (five) years after the last date defined in clause 3.2 or (ii) until the Confidential Information may reasonably be considered as no longer confidential.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase 'but in all cases using a professional degree of care' allows for a potentially negligent level of security, conflicting with reasonable standards of data protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 32 of the GDPR requires implementing appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk, including consideration of the state of the art, the costs of implementation and the nature, scope, context and purposes of processing as well as the risk of varying likelihood and severity for the rights and freedoms of natural persons. By allowing parties to determine their own level of care, the modified text could lead to insufficient security measures that violate GDPR if personal data is involved.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Art. 32 GDPR – Security of processing - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability Taking into account the state of the art, the costs of implementation and the nature, scope, context and purposes of processing as well as the risk of varying likelihood and severity for the rights and freedoms of natural persons, the controller and the processor shall implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk, including inter alia as appropriate: the pseudonymisation and encryption of personal data; the ability to ensure the ongoing confidentiality, integrity, availability and resilience of processing systems and services; the ability to restore the availability and access to personal data in a timely manner in the event of a physical or technical incident; a process for regularly testing, assessing and evaluating the effectiveness of technical and organisational measures for ensuring the security of the processing. In assessing the appropriate level of security account shall be taken in particular of the risks that are presented by processing, in particular from accidental or unlawful destruction, loss, alteration, unauthorised disclosure of, or access to personal data transmitted, stored or otherwise processed. Adherence to an approved code of conduct as referred to in Article 40 or an approved certification mechanism as referred to in Article 42 may be used as an element by which to demonstrate compliance with the requirements set out in paragraph 1 of this Article. The controller and processor shall take steps to ensure that any natural person acting under the authority of the controller or the processor who has access to personal data does not process them except on instructions from the controller, unless he or she is required to do so by Union or Member State law. Suitable Recitals ( 75 ) Risks to the Rights and Freedoms of Natural Person

perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates 'a professional degree of care' in securing confidential information, while the changed text allows each party to use 'whatever degree of care it deems appropriate.' This introduces a legal contradiction because it permits a level of security that might fall below legally required standards. GDPR Article 32, as presented in Scraped Law Snippet 1, explicitly requires 'appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk,' considering factors like 'the state of the art' and 'the costs of implementation.' By removing the 'professional degree of care' standard, the modified contract could lead to security measures deemed insufficient under GDPR, especially if personal data is involved. The change directly weakens the security obligation, potentially violating GDPR requirements for data protection.

perturbation.original_text: 2.2. Governing Law and Venue . All aspects of this Agreement, all matters arising from it, including disputes or lawsuits, and the relationship of the Parties relating to this Agreement, are governed by the laws of Luxembourg , and its courts shall have exclusive jurisdiction thereover . Nevertheless, each Party recognizes that a breach of confidentiality may cause irreparable harm to the other and that actual damages may be difficult to ascertain or inadequate in such cases. Each Party shall thus have the right to enforce this Agreemen t by injunction, specific performance, or other equitable relief, without a requirement to prove actual damages and without prejudice to any other rights and remedies that they may have otherwise.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.2. Governing Law and Venue . All aspects of this Agreement, all matters arising from it, including disputes or lawsuits, and the relationship of the Parties relating to this Agreement, are governed by the laws of Luxembourg, and its courts shall have exclusive jurisdiction thereover. Nevertheless, each Party recognizes that a breach of confidentiality may cause irreparable harm to the other and that actual damages may be difficult to ascertain or inadequate in such cases. Each Party shall thus have the right to enforce this Agreement only through monetary damages, precluding injunctive relief.
perturbation.explanation: By limiting the remedies to only monetary damages, the contract contradicts established principles allowing for equitable relief like injunctions in cases of irreparable harm, undermining the potential for effective enforcement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Article 655 of the Luxembourg Code of Civil Procedure.
perturbation.law_citation: Article 655 of the Luxembourg Code of Civil Procedure
perturbation.law_explanation: Although the first link is not directly to the article in question, but rather to the whole code (in French), it is impractical to obtain the single page in French. The article describes judicial remedies available in the instance of breach, which would include specific performance. The modification to the contract directly contradicts this available remedy, preventing the party the full breadth of options available under Luxembourg law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Legilux Merci d'avoir rejoint le site du Journal Officiel; celui-ci n'est disponible qu'avec un navigateur supportant javascript. Afin de profiter d'une expérience maximale sur ce site, nous vous conseillons d'utiliser un navigateur récent.
perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly limits the available remedies to only monetary damages, precluding injunctive relief. The Law Explanation states that Luxembourg law includes specific performance as a judicial remedy. Scraped Law Snippet 1 indicates it is from an official journal, implying legal authority, but it does not provide legal text. However, the Law Explanation supports the idea that the modification contradicts available remedies.

perturbation.original_text: 1.1. “Authorized Persons ” means a person or entity that a Party or a Party’s Group Company authorizes to access the Confidential Information for the Purpose, and who is bound by confidentiality terms at least as protective of the Confidential Information as this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1. “Authorized Persons” means a person or entity that a Party or a Party’s Group Company authorizes to access the Confidential Information for the Purpose, regardless of whether they are bound by confidentiality terms.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the requirement for confidentiality terms for Authorized Persons, the definition allows for disclosure of Confidential Information without protection, significantly weakening the agreement's core purpose.
perturbation.law_explanation: This conflicts with the spirit and intent of trade secret protection laws, which require reasonable measures to protect the secrecy of confidential information. Authorizing persons without binding confidentiality terms fails to meet this requirement, potentially voiding trade secret status.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.— (1) In general.— An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.— (A) In general.— (i) Application.— Based on an affidavit or verified complaint satisfying the requirements of this paragraph, the court may, upon ex parte application but only in extraordinary circumstances, issue an order providing for the seizure of property necessary to prevent the propagation or dissemination of the trade secret that is the subject of the action. (ii) Requirements for issuing order.— The court may not grant an application under clause (i) unless the court finds that it clearly appears from specific facts that— (I) an order issued pursuant to Rule 65 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure or another form of equitable relief would be inadequate to achieve the purpose of this paragraph because the party to which the order would be issued would evade, avoid, or otherwise not comply with such an order; (II) an immediate and irreparable injury will occur if such seizure is not ordered; (III) the harm to the applicant of denying the application outweighs the harm to the legitimate interests of the person against whom seizure would be ordered of granting the application and substantially outweighs the harm to any third parties who may be harmed by such seizure; (IV) the applicant is likely to succeed in showing that— (aa) the information is a trade secret ; and (bb) the person against whom seizure would be ordered— (AA

perturbation.justification: The modified contract language directly contradicts the intent of trade secret protection laws, as it removes the requirement for confidentiality agreements for authorized personnel accessing confidential information. Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to actions taken against misappropriation of trade secrets and actions to prevent the dissemination of trade secrets. By removing the confidentiality requirement, the changed text fails to reasonably protect the secrecy of confidential information, increasing the risk of misappropriation, which is contrary to the requirements for trade secret protection. This falls under the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction' because the contractual language deviates from legally recognized expectations for trade secret protection.
